one_line_takeaway: By exploiting the fragility of jailbreak prompts, embedding-level noise injected at the last token -- guided by a small set of model-specific 'anchor tokens' discovered via brute-force analysis -- lets an LLM re-trigger its own built-in refusal behavior as a jailbreak detector, cutting ASR (e.g., 60% to 29% for AutoDAN-Turbo on Qwen2.5) with little to no utility loss, though effectiveness and black-box transferability both drop under the strongest and adaptive attacks.
key_finding: Embedding-level noise injected at the last token is the most effective and controllable disruption point (better than input-text-level or intermediate hidden-state injection, and better than harmful-token/fictitious-token/random-token targeting alone); successful disruption embeddings collapse onto a small, model-specific, long-tailed set of 'anchor tokens' (top-1 anchor ~52% of cases, top-3 ~90%), and this structure lets a guided two-stage search reach detection rates the paper reports as reaching ~90% DR within 50 search iterations, and reach 90% of final DR with far fewer average search calls than brute force (e.g., Random Search needs only 1.28-4.55 average iterations across Vicuna/LLaMA2/Qwen2.5 to reach 90% of final DR vs. 1.57-5.62 for brute force). On the main defense benchmark (Table 1), the method drives ASR to 0-11% across GCG/PAIR/RS/I-FSJ on Vicuna-13B and LLaMA2-7B-Chat (vs. baselines like RESTA leaving 2-44% ASR and SmoothLLM leaving 0-68%), and against the strongest attack, AutoDAN-Turbo on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, reduces ASR from 60% (vanilla) to 29% while preserving model utility (Alpaca 68.7, IFEval 61.6, identical to vanilla, versus RESTA's utility drop to 59.6/57.0 and SmoothLLM's drop to 17.2/31.3). Detection Rate/False-alarm Rate results (Table 3) show DR of 0.52-1.0 depending on attack/model with FR of 0-0.02 on benign IFEval/Alpaca prompts. Anchor tokens transfer within a model family without re-derivation: anchor tokens found on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct applied directly to Qwen3-8B still achieved an 87% defense success rate. Black-box many-shot transfer substantially beats one-shot transfer (e.g., GPT-4.1 DR against AutoDAN-Turbo: 0.56 many-shot vs 0.18 one-shot; Gemini-2.5: 0.76 many-shot vs 0.22 one-shot).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is a white-box (and transferable black-box) jailbreak DETECTION method for LLMs that exploits an empirically observed 'fragility' property of successful jailbreak prompts: because token-level (GCG) and prompt-level (PAIR) jailbreak methods require multiple rounds of iterative refinement to find a working prompt, successful jailbreak prompts occupy small, isolated regions of prompt space, so small perturbations tend to push them back into a 'denial response' region, whereas perturbing benign prompts does not. Building on this, the authors (1) run brute-force sweeps over injection layer, target token, perturbed dimensions, and noise strength (||delta||_2 in [-30,30], step 0.05) to characterize which noise configurations elicit denial; (2) discover that embeddings which successfully flip a jailbreak prompt to denial cluster (via a nearest-token 'emb2token' operation) around a small set of model-specific 'anchor tokens' with a long-tailed distribution (top-1 anchor accounts for ~52% of successful cases, top-3 for ~90%); and (3) use this to build a two-stage noise-search algorithm (anchor-guided search, falling back to a randomized/shrinking-interval search) that finds an appropriate embedding-level noise far more efficiently than brute force. At inference, a prompt is classified as a jailbreak if some noise within a search budget flips the model's output to a denial response, otherwise it is treated as benign. The method is also extended to a black-box setting via 'disruption transfer': disrupted embeddings from a white-box surrogate model are mapped back to words (top-K nearest tokens, many-shot) and tested against a black-box target model.
threat_model: Adversary crafts jailbreak prompts against LLMs with built-in RLHF-based safety alignment, using either token-level optimization attacks (GCG) or prompt-level/LLM-assisted attacks (PAIR, Random Search variant of GCG, I-FSJ, AutoDAN-Turbo) to elicit harmful outputs. The defense assumes white-box access to token embeddings of the model being protected (self-transfer) for the primary method, and separately evaluates a black-box variant where the defender only has surrogate-model access and must transfer disrupted prompts to an unseen target model (GPT-4.1, Gemini-2.5). The paper additionally evaluates 'adaptive attacks' in which the attacker is assumed to know the defense exists and adapts PAIR/AutoDAN-Turbo to explicitly try to bypass it.
limitations: The authors' own reported weaknesses: (1) defense effectiveness degrades noticeably against the strongest attack, AutoDAN-Turbo (residual ASR up to 26-29% even with their defense, versus near-0% against GCG/PAIR/I-FSJ), indicating the fragility assumption holds less cleanly for that attack; (2) under adaptive attacks (Table 2) where the attacker explicitly targets the deployed defense, ASR only partially drops (e.g., 33% for PAIR-adaptive on Vicuna-13B vs. 2% for non-adaptive PAIR), so the defense is not fully adaptive-attack-robust; (3) the primary method requires white-box embedding access, and the black-box extension via 'disruption transfer' has markedly lower detection rates than the white-box self-transfer setting (e.g., one-shot transfer to GPT-4.1/Gemini-2.5 achieves only 0.18-0.33 DR, requiring the more expensive many-shot variant to reach 0.56-0.83 DR); (4) the anchor-token identification step itself required brute-force search over 30 seed prompts at ~1 GPU-hour per prompt, a nontrivial offline cost that must be repeated per model (though the authors show family-level reuse works, e.g. Qwen2.5-to-Qwen3); (5) the Impact Statement explicitly flags that stronger defenses may incentivize more adaptive/stealthy jailbreak strategies, an acknowledged arms-race limitation rather than a solved problem.
